title: What is a Long-Range Dependency?
concept: A long-range dependency occurs when a word late in a sentence depends on context introduced far earlier (e.g., matching plural subjects to verbs across multiple clauses).

exercises.prompt: Why do words in a sentence need to "connect" with each other?

options.text: They depend on each other for correct grammar.
They are always spelled the same way.
They must always be right next to each other.

exercises.explain: In language, words often depend on each other for meaning (like matching a singular subject with a singular verb).

cards.title: Lost in the Middle
cards.body: When you read a long sentence, how do you remember the beginning by the time you reach the end? AIs struggle with this too! Let's find out how computers bridge the gap between words that are far apart.

cards.title: Bridging the Gap
cards.body: A long-range dependency happens when two parts of a sentence are closely related, but a long stream of other words sits between them. AI models have to remember the first part to make sense of the second part!

cards.title: The Long-Distance Key
cards.body: Imagine reading a book where a mystery is set up in Chapter 1 ("The key is gold..."), but the locked box isn't found until Chapter 10. To open the box, you must remember the color of the key from way back at the start. Your brain has to hold onto that information across many pages!

cards.title: A Long Sentence Example
cards.body:
Look at this sentence: "The **dog** that chased the three cats, ran through the muddy park, and barked at the mailman **is** brown." 

Even though "mailman" is close to "is", the verb "is" must agree with the singular "dog" from the very beginning.

cards.title: The Neighbor Myth
cards.body: It's easy to think that computers only need to look at the words right next to each other to understand a sentence. But if an AI did that, it would get confused by long sentences and match the wrong words together!

cards.title: Short vs. Long Dependencies
cards.body: See how the length of a sentence changes how far the AI has to look to connect the subject and the verb.

right.text: The boy who wore red shoes and rode his bike runs. (Separated by 7 words)

left.text: The boy runs. (Subject and verb are next to each other)

exercises.prompt: In the sentence: "The books on the dusty wooden shelf are blue." Which word does the verb "are" depend on?
exercises.explain: The word "are" must match the plural "books" from the beginning, even though the singular "shelf" is closer to the verb.

exercises.prompt: Is it true that long-range dependencies are difficult for simple AI models because the key words are separated by many other words?
exercises.explain: Long-range dependencies are hard because the AI must remember information across many steps without getting distracted by nearby words.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe this word-gap phenomenon.
exercises.sentence: When a verb at the end of a sentence depends on a subject at the very beginning, we call this a long-range [blank].
exercises.explain: When two words depend on each other but are separated by many other words, we call it a long-range dependency.

exercises.prompt: Which AI model correctly resolves the long-range dependency between the subject and the verb?

options.text: Model A: "The cat that watched the active birds sleep in the yard."
Model B: "The cat that watched the active birds sleeps in the yard."
Model C: "The cat that watched the active birds sleeping in the yard."

exercises.explain: AI Model B is correct because the singular subject "cat" matches the singular verb "sleeps," despite the plural "birds" in between. Model A got confused by the nearby word.

exercises.prompt: Which of these sentences contains the longest-range dependency between the subject (kids) and its action verb (run)?

options.text: The kids who played all day in the yard sleep.
The young kids who wore green shirts and played in the muddy yard run.

exercises.explain: The phrase "The young kids" is plural and starts the sentence, but the action "run" doesn't appear until the very end, separated by a long description. The model must connect these distant words to make grammatical sense.
